summary: A lightweight, single-threaded Python command-line tool that reads CSV files line-by-line, validates structure and content, converts rows to JSON objects, and outputs either to stdout or a file. Designed for correctness, observability, and resilience with minimal dependencies.

components.name: CLI Interface
components.responsibility: Parse command-line arguments, display help/usage, orchestrate workflow, and handle user input validation
interfaces: accepts --input, --output, --help; emits parsed args to Converter

components.name: CSV Reader
components.responsibility: Stream-read CSV files row-by-row using csv.DictReader, detect encoding issues, report line numbers for errors
interfaces: reads from file path; yields validated rows or raises RowValidationError

components.name: Validator
components.responsibility: Enforce CSV structural integrity: column count consistency, non-empty header, field emptiness checks per row
interfaces: receives rows and header; returns ValidationResult with errors or clean data

components.name: JSON Serializer
components.responsibility: Convert validated rows into JSON-serializable dictionaries and write formatted output to file or stdout
interfaces: accepts list of dicts or iterator; writes to file or sys.stdout with indent=2

components.name: Logger
components.responsibility: Emit structured debug/info/error logs with timestamps and context (e.g., line number, file path)
interfaces: log_error(), log_info(), log_debug() called by all components

deployment_strategy: Distribute as installable Python package via PyPI; support pip install; include entry point script; no containerization or orchestration required.
security_considerations: No network I/O — no injection or remote code execution vectors; validate file paths to prevent directory traversal (e.g., normalize and restrict to absolute/relative safe paths); avoid eval/exec; use csv module instead of manual parsing to prevent injection via delimiter escaping.
scalability_notes: Optimized for memory efficiency via streaming (no full-file load); supports CSV files up to ~1GB on typical hardware; horizontal scaling not applicable (single-process CLI); performance scales linearly with file size; no concurrency needed per requirements.
